title: `podman wait` doesn't always distinguish "exited" "stopped" "created" as different conditions
body:
### Issue Description

Utilizing the `podman wait` command doesn't always have the intended effect. According to the docs:

> After all conditions are satisfied, the containersâ€™ return codes are printed

So the command should block until the condition is met (excluding the edge case of a container not existing). There are many cases where the command will return without the condition being met (see steps to reproduce).

### Steps to reproduce the issue

Steps to reproduce the issue
1. Run `podman create --name init_2 busybox sh -c "sleep 15 && echo Init says Hi"`
2. Run `podman inspect init_2 | grep "Status"`
3. Run `podman wait --condition=exited init_2` (Ctrl+C if terminal hangs)
4. Run `podman wait --condition=stopped init_2` (Ctrl+C if terminal hangs)
5. Run `podman wait --condition=created init_2` (Ctrl+C if terminal hangs)

### Describe the results you received

The `podman inspect` command will show `"Status": "created"`. Each of the other `podman wait` commands will return immediately without blocking.

### Describe the results you expected

`podman inspect` will show `"Status": "created"`. Waiting for "exited" or "stopped" should block while only the condition "created" should return immediately.

### podman info output

comments.body:
I reproduced the issue on upstream. The `exited` and `stopped` are states that have a special waiting condition, which is to wait for the container's terminal state. In this case, the container is only configured, so I am not sure how this should behave.

WDYT? How should this behave? @matejvasek @mheon

The `Created` bit is understandable. Podman's internal states do not map 1:1 to Docker's equivalents, so `podman ps` and `podman inspect` do some conversions to provide Docker-compatible output. However, we're not doing the reverse on `podman wait`. Internally, the container is stuck in `ContainerStateConfigured`, and waiting for `ContainerStateCreated`, which is what would happen when the container is created i the OCI runtime when it starts. Externally, we display both `ContainerStateCreated` and `ContainerStateConfigured` as `Created` which creates confusion. IMO, easiest fix there is to have `--condition=created` imply waiting for either `ContainerStateCreated` or `ContainerStateConfigured`, to match what we're outputting.

For the other two: I think we need to figure out what Docker is doing and match that. I have a feeling that the current behavior - treating any non-running container as stopped/exited - was added because Docker does the same thing... In which case the proper thing would be to document this in the manpages and retain current behavior.

> For the other two: I think we need to figure out what Docker is doing and match that. I have a feeling that the current behavior - treating any non-running container as stopped/exited - was added because Docker does the same thing... In which case the proper thing would be to document this in the manpages and retain current behavior.

I am pretty sure this is correct, the behavior for exited is intentional. Any non running container must mean exit right away and not hang. AFAIK there is an internal `next-exit` state in the docker compat API implemented via events that would likely be what the reporter wants then.

The wait condition names in the docker API certainly make more sense https://docs.docker.com/reference/api/engine/version/v1.51/#tag/Container/operation/ContainerWait
`not-running`, `next-exit`, `removed`.

so `exited` matches `not-running` basically
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
